coredata.dc:title: pH-Responsive strips integrated with resazurin and carbon dots for monitoring shrimp freshness
coredata.dc:creator: Ezati, Parya
Khan, Ajahar
Rhim, Jong-Whan
Kim, Jun Tae
Molaei, Rahim
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal approach using tangerine peel (Tan) and resazurin (Res) to fabricate biocompatible indicators for food freshness. The CDs' pH-responsive mechanism, morphology, zeta potential, XPS, and optical and fluorescence analysis were investigated. The as-prepared tangerine peel/resazurin carbon dots (Tan/Res CDs) exhibited pH-responsive emission that changed from yellow to orange as the pH value increased. The Tan/Res CDs showed the sensing ability of ammonia with a detection limit of 0.84 μM by proportionally losing fluorescence intensity as the concentration increased from 1 to 100 μM. The CDs were coated onto paper strips to impart biogenic amine (BAs) detection for pH-responsive intelligent monitoring of packaged foods. The Tan/Res CDs paper-based indicator exhibited an impressive color change from yellow to brown during the detection of ammonia vapor. The indicator also showed the ability to detect BAs through a color change, demonstrating the ability to monitor the freshness of shrimp in situ. Additionally, the efficacy of the Tan/Res CDs indicator is validated by total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N), providing customers and suppliers with a simple, inexpensive, and portable tool to monitor the freshness of seafood in real-time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Resazurin
PH-responsive color indicator
Food freshness
Ammonia

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of indicators for shrimp packaging
Application of indicators for shrimp packaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) grant funded by the Korean government ( MSIT ) (No. 2022R1A2B02001422 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of (a) CDs synthesis, (b) preparation of CDs-based paper indicators, and (c) their application on shrimp packaging.

(a,b) TEM and size histogram with a curve fitted to the data using a Gaussian model, (c) FTIR spectra and (d) XPS of Tan and Tan/Res CDs, and high-resolution scans of Tan/Res CDs for (e) C1s, (f) O1s, and (g) N1s.
EDX results of (a) Tan and (b) Tan/Res CDs.

(a) Photographs of Tan, Res, and Tan/Res CDs exposed to different buffer solutions under daylight and UV light. Fluorescence emission spectra of the (b) Tan, (c) Res, and (d) Tan/Res CDs at different pH.

Fluorescence spectra of Tan/Res CDs at various concentrations of NH3 (a) and plot of the quenching intensity of Tan/Res CDs versus NH3 concentration (b). Plausible illustration of the responsive color change of (c) (i) Tan CDs, (ii) Res, and (iii) Tan/Res CDs towards NH3.
Photographs of Tan, Res, and Tan/Res CDs indicators exposed to different buffer solutions with their color values.
Photographs of Tan, Res, and Tan/Res CDs indicators exposed to ammonia and acid vapors with their color values.
Photograph of shrimp freshness monitoring by Tan, Res, Tan/Res CDs indicators.
Changes in the color values of the indicators and the pH and TVB-N values of shrimp samples after storage at 25 °C for 24 h.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The data are presented as a mean ± SD. Different symbols and letters followed by each value indicate a significant difference (p < 0.05) between each indicator's values.

head.ce:title: pH-Responsive strips integrated with resazurin and carbon dots for monitoring shrimp freshness

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal approach using tangerine peel (Tan) and resazurin (Res) to fabricate biocompatible indicators for food freshness. The CDs' pH-responsive mechanism, morphology, zeta potential, XPS, and optical and fluorescence analysis were investigated. The as-prepared tangerine peel/resazurin carbon dots (Tan/Res CDs) exhibited pH-responsive emission that changed from yellow to orange as the pH value increased. The Tan/Res CDs showed the sensing ability of ammonia with a detection limit of 0.84 μM by proportionally losing fluorescence intensity as the concentration increased from 1 to 100 μM. The CDs were coated onto paper strips to impart biogenic amine (BAs) detection for pH-responsive intelligent monitoring of packaged foods. The Tan/Res CDs paper-based indicator exhibited an impressive color change from yellow to brown during the detection of ammonia vapor. The indicator also showed the ability to detect BAs through a color change, demonstrating the ability to monitor the freshness of shrimp in situ. Additionally, the efficacy of the Tan/Res CDs indicator is validated by total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N), providing customers and suppliers with a simple, inexpensive, and portable tool to monitor the freshness of seafood in real-time.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food poisoning caused by ingesting contaminated food, deterioration, and loss of food quality during storage and distribution is a major concern in the food industry and supply chains [1,2]. In general, the food supply chain starts from the farm to the consumer's table, and in the process, contamination of microorganisms and various chemicals poses a serious risk to human health, resulting in the disease and death of millions of people each year [3,4]. Further, biogenic amines (BAs) are low molecular weight nitrogen-containing compounds produced in the process of preserving and processing numerous foods such as meat, fish, and wine, and when consumed in excess, physiological side effects such as headache, stomach pain, and vomiting may appear [5–7]. Therefore, a safe food supply can greatly improve human health and quality of life. From a food safety point of view, many effective techniques, such as gas/liquid chromatography, enzyme-linked methodologies, and immunological detection approaches, are used to identify food ingredients and hazards [8,9]. However, most of these techniques have been restricted to the practical detection of food samples due to their inherent disadvantages of complex sample treatment and being time-consuming [10]. Despite their high sensitivity and selectivity for determining various analytes to ensure food safety, these techniques call for pricey equipment and qualified personnel. As a result, there is still a large gap between theoretical studies and practical analyte detection [11]. So far, numerous sensors have been created to overcome this difficulty in food detection. Optical sensors have been suggested because of their excellent selectivity and sensitivity [12].

BAs, widely used as a quality indicator for meat products, can be detected using optical sensors, including organic molecules and metal nanoparticles [13]. However, the detection of BAs is restricted by using chromogenic probes in organic or inorganic/aqueous solutions under numerous tight circumstances [14]. Therefore, there is a need to use innovative, non-toxic, and inexpensive biometric amine-reactive optical sensors. Recently, developing eco-friendly and safe intelligent indicators based on natural resources has been an emergent research domain for analytical scientists [15]. Of these practical indicators, the design of a pH-sensitive indicator is more suitable because of its compact, highly responsive, and distinct visual color change characteristics. Resazurin (Res) is a non-toxic and faintly luminous purple compound frequently used to evaluate cell viability [16]. Res turns into an intensely fluorescent derivative resorufin (pink) when exposed to a redox reaction [17]. Resorufin forms a caproyl ester under acidic conditions, rendering the colorless and non-fluorescent hexanoyl resorufin. This caproyl ester group reacts with ammonia and BAs that release highly fluorescent pink color resorufin [18]. As a result, Res may attract much attention, especially in developing pH sensors to investigate its applicability in smart food packaging. Although Res has background fluorescence, it may be difficult to notice subtle changes in acidic/basic environments or the presence of BAs, making it unfavorable for practical applications where a clear identification is required. Therefore, it is important to create portable chromophore or fluorescence sensors with high contrast ratio for non-contact and visual detection of BAs gas.

With the recent development of nanotechnology, various nanomaterials such as metal nanoparticles, semiconductors, and carbon-based materials are being used to develop sensors that can detect changes in food quality [19,20]. Among these nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) with an ultrafine diameter of less than 10 nm are hailed as promising materials for sensing applications [21–23]. Attempts have been made to synthesize CDs with an inexpensive and renewable basic material supply. In this regard, biowaste materials can be viable and potentially replace fossil fuels as feedstock for various chemicals [24]. One of the world's least utilized natural resources and geographically diverse biowaste residues is tangerine peel (Tan), and high value-added utilization of this enormous residue is encouraging in terms of reducing agricultural waste and effective use of underutilized natural resources [25]. Tan comprises carbohydrates, including cellulose, glucose, fructose, and sucrose [26]. Researchers have found that some chemicals bind to CD and consequently quench the fluorescence [27]. In particular, luminescent CDs are used as fluorescence-sensing materials for detecting various compounds in foodstuffs [28]. The detection performance of these CDs is well-established in many cases. For example, N-doped CDs prepared using N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) for pH sensing exhibited linearly pH-dependent photoluminescence activity from pH 1.5–7.5 [29]. Fu et al. showed that CDs-containing gelatin/chitosan films could indicate the freshness of perishable foods such as fish [13]. Taking advantage of the properties of pH-sensitive CDs with a narrow response range, we constructed an innovative fluorescent CDs indicator using the Tan and Res for facile, fast, and inexpensive detection of BAs. The Tan/Res CDs are considered more favorable for alkaline BAs detection as it exhibits a more pronounced orange color in alkaline conditions. Therefore, Tan/Res CDs were made into paper strips to monitor the shrimp's freshness and investigate their performance since various BAs are generated during shrimp storage.

Tangerine peel (Tan) and resazurin (Res) were used to prepare Tan/Res CDs using a one-step hydrothermal method, as shown schematically in Fig. 1a [21]. For this, 0.3 g of Tan powder and 0.001 g of Res were dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water while stirring and added in a 100 mL stainless steel reactor with an inner surface surrounded by polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) and reacted at 140 °C for 6 h. After the reaction product was cooled and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove unreacted components, the supernatant was filtered through a Whatman membrane filter (25 mm diameter, 0.2 µm pore size, Germany) to remove impurities. Before further testing, the filtrate was stored in the refrigerator (4 °C). Pure Tangerine peel CDs (Tan CDs) were also made separately using the same method for comparison purposes. According to the substances, the developed CDs were categorized as Tan CDs, Res, and Tan/Res CDs. The produced CDs were characterized using the methods provided in the Supporting Information.

The fabricated CDs solution was applied to Whatman No. 42 filter paper using the drop coating deposition method to develop the paper indicator (Fig. 1b). Briefly, filter paper (indicator carrier) was cut into 3.0 cm × 3.0 cm squares, 1.0 mL of the indicator solution was added dropwise to the indicator carrier, and dried in air. Once the coated indicator carrier has dried, 1.0 mL of the indicator solution is added again and allowed to dry overnight at room temperature. The paper indicator tags were then stored in the refrigerator (>10 °C) for further analysis. The developed indicators were characterized using the methods provided in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of indicators for shrimp packaging
ce:section.ce:para: The placement of the pH indicators on the sterilized packaging boxes (100 cm × 100 cm × 5.5 cm) is schematically depicted in Fig. 1c. The pH-sensitive indicators were placed on the inner side of the packaging lid, and 100 g of fresh samples were packed into the boxes. The shrimp samples were then kept at 25 °C for 24 h. The technique described in the color property section was also used to measure the color of the attached pH-sensitive indicator in three replicates.

The chemical structure and composition of the as-prepared CDs were characterized by TEM, surface zeta potential, EDX, FTIR, and XPS analyses. The hierarchical morphology and size distribution of synthesized CDs were carried out by TEM images presented in Fig. 2a,b. The TEM images verified that the Tan and Tan/Res derived CDs were spherical with the size distribution (>10 nm) in the quantum range as per the previous report [30]. Both CDs were observed to be in uniform particle sizes with diameters ranging from 2.08 to 2.34 nm and 2.19–2.86 nm for Tan and Tan/Res CDs, respectively. Fig. 2a,b show histograms for the diameter of Tan and Tan/Res-based CDs, assessed by examination of TEM images sited at different locations. The engineered mean diameters of both Tan and Tan/Res-based CDs were found to be ∼3 ± 0.4 and ∼3 ± 0.5 nm, respectively, based on fitting the histogram to a Gaussian model.

FTIR spectroscopy was used to determine the functional group types on the synthesized CDs' surface by providing information about the compounds in Tan used as the primary carbon precursor to making the CDs. Fig. 2c illustrates the FTIR spectra of Tan and Tan/R CDs. The main characteristic peaks centered around 3350–3500 cm−1 ascribed the vibration of -O-H and -N-H bonds stretching; peaks around 2935 and 2346 cm−1 indicated C-H vibration of alkyl and alkenes groups; the peaks at 1760 cm−1 attributed to the CO stretching mode of the carbonyl group; the peaks around 1620 and 1505 cm−1 indicated to the vibrations of CC in the aromatic group; the band at 1370 cm−1 the vibrations of C-O in the carboxylate group; the bands at around 1264 cm−1 indicated C-N tensile vibrations; 1017 cm−1 belonged to the C-O groups in polysaccharides; and the peak at 810 cm−1 attributed to the stretching of C-H bond in the benzene ring [31,32]. For both Tan and Tan/Res CDs, multiple peaks around 1600–1500 cm−1 belonged to CC vibrations in the aromatic groups [33]. Fig. 2c indicates changes in FTIR spectra of the Tan/Res CDs compared to the neat Tan CDs. This analysis shows that the intensity of most of the peaks due to Res either diminished or disappeared in Tan/Res composition corresponding to the bands at around 3000–3500 and 2346 cm−1 indicates the vibration of -O-H and -N-H stretching, and C-H vibration of alkenes groups. The reason for this is that the stretching vibration of most of the functional groups (-OH, C-H, CC, and N-H) overlapped in the same region.

The chemical composition of the CDs was then investigated using XPS. Fig. 2d and Table S2 display the XPS spectra, binding energy levels (eV), and element concentration as a percentage of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2d, the XPS full-survey spectrum had three peaks at 292.2 eV, 398.99, and 534.9 eV, indicating that C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s are the main constituting elements of Tan CDs. The typical O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s photoelectron peaks from oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon are seen in the broad survey spectra of the Tan/Res CDs. Further, the composition of Res into Tan/Res impart nitrogen-based functional groups onto CDs surface, which is confirmed by the appearance of an additional peak of N (Fig. 2d). Three peaks can be identified in the high-resolution spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 2e), with binding energies of 284.7 eV for C-C/CC, 289 eV for C-N/CC-N/C-O, and 288.3 eV for CO/CN [7]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 f) exhibits two peaks at 531.1 and 532.1 eV, respectively, corresponding to COOH/CO and C-O groups [34]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 2 g) can be decomposed into three peaks with binding energies of 398.9, 399.2, and 400.5 eV, respectively, which correspond to CN, C-N, and N-H bands [35]. These findings suggested that the FTIR and XPS spectra were consistent, revealing that hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amide groups existed on the surfaces of Tan/Res CDs.

The zeta potential of the CDs prepared using Tan CDs was − 12.9 mV, and that of the CDs prepared using Tan/Res was − 14.5 mV (Table S1, Supporting Information). In general, it is known that particles exhibit excellent stability in aqueous dispersions when the zeta potential value is close to or more than ± 15 mV [36]. On the other hand, when the absolute value of the zeta potential value decreases, the repulsive force becomes greater than the attraction between the particles, and the dispersion of the particles tends to collapse and aggregate [37]. The zeta potential test result indicated that the combined use of Tan and Res increased the colloidal suspension stability of the Tan/Res CDs.

EDX analysis was performed to confirm the presence of characteristic elemental peaks constituting Tan and Tan/Res CDs ( Fig. 3a,b). The EDX spectrum of Tan CDs shows strong carbon and oxygen element peaks. However, Tan/Res CDs show an additional N peak, indicating successful encapsulation by Res (Fig. 3b).

The optical characteristics of the synthesized CDs were evaluated by UV–vis spectrophotometry. Fig. 4a,c exhibit the UV–vis spectra for Tan, Res, and Tan/Res CDs. The UV–vis spectra of Tan and Tan/Res CDs were consistent with those prepared using Inoki mushrooms [30]. The pale-yellow and the dark-yellow aqueous solution of Tan and Tan/Res CDs emitted strong green light under full band UV irradiation because of the quantum confinement (right inset, Fig. 4a,c). However, the pink aqueous solution of Res remains the same in both normal and under UV light (right inset, Fig. 4b). The instant color change in the presence of UV-irradiation is the characteristic phenomenon of CDs, where the carbon source and preparation route adopted to synthesize CDs depict the intensity of the color change [38].

Moreover, for Tan and Tan/Res CDs, absorption peaks around ∼295 nm indicated the n-π * transitions of CO and C-OH bonds, and weak shouldering peaks around ∼280 nm displayed π-π * transitions of C-C bonds with sp2 hybridization (Fig. 4a,c) [38]. These surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peaks are assigned to the carbonization of Tan and Tan/Res and the successful fabrication of CDs. However, the UV–visible spectrum of Res possessed intense absorption peaks around ∼300 and ∼592 nm and a weak lump around ∼380 nm, similar to the Res spectrum reported elsewhere [39]. These characteristic peaks for Res are ascribed to the π-π * transition of the phenoxazin-3-one, and the weak peak indicates n-π * transitions of the N-oxide, respectively. The fluorescent characteristics of the synthesized CDs were determined through fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4d,f, the Tan and Tan/Res derived CDs demonstrate an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. Both types of CDs exhibited instance and broad emission peaks; the emission response was tunable and reliant on the excitation wavelength [40]. It was also observed that as the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 to 440 nm, a clear and gradual bathochromic (red) shift was noticed in the emission peaks (Fig. 4c). This exclusive feature is probably due to the synthesized CDs' surface defects, which form tunable emissions with different energy states [30].

In addition, other functional groups may also affect the tunable emission response. The maximum fluorescent spectra intensity for both CDs was recorded at about 430 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. However, Fig. 4b indicates that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of Res are located at about 300 nm and 590 nm, respectively [39]. When compared to CD's fluorescent spectra, Res showed no excitation-dependent fluorescence response. As shown in Fig. 4e, a bathochromic shift was not observed with the increase in the excitation wavelength.

It can also be seen from Fig. 4d,f that negligible differences in the shape and intensity of emission peak shifts were observed at excitation wavelengths of ∼300 and ∼460 nm for Tan and Tan/Res CDs, which is most likely due to the size change and quantum confinement effects due to the encapsulation of Tan with Res. Even slight modifications of the quantum domain are known to result in quantum confinement effects and substantial changes in photoluminescence (PL) emission [41]. Although previous studies have confirmed that doping of CDs also has a significant effect on luminescence, the exact PL mechanism that accounts for the excitation wavelength-dependent emission is still unclear and remains to be recognized.

In our preliminary tests, 0.001 g of Res exhibited a useful color change at a pH value of 7, so the same amount was used to prepare the proposed indicator. Fig. 5a shows the color variations of CD solutions at various pH levels in daylight and UV light. Under daylight, the Tan CDs solutions showed no color change under acidic conditions (pH = 4–6), but they were slightly yellowish at pH 7 and more intense at pH 10. However, the Res and Tan/Res CD solutions differ in color between pH 4 and 10. At pH 7, the Res solution exhibited a noticeable pink color and remained steady as the pH was raised to more alkaline levels. Changes in the chemical structure of Res create color shifts at various pH levels. However, Tan/Res CDs started yellow and turned orange as the pH increased. From pH 7–9, this color became intense. The Tan/Res CDs solution exhibited the most intense orange tint at pH 10. Under UV light, the Tan CDs solution did not change color at acidic conditions (pH = 4–6), but the Res solution showed a pink emission from pH 7–10, which was stronger in the alkaline environment. For Tan/Res CDs, a more intense green color appeared in the pH 4–6 range and changed to red emission in the alkaline pH range under UV light. The complex chemical structure of Tan/Res CDs due to the interaction between Res and Tan is presumed to be the cause of this predicament. In addition, the vivid color change of Tan/Res CDs makes them more suitable for detecting food spoilage.

The fluorescence spectra of the CD solutions at various pH are shown in Fig. 5b-d. The Tan CDs solution showed a fluorescence peak at about 430 nm, resulting in yellow color. As the pH of the solution increased in an acidic environment (pH 4–5), the fluorescence peak steadily increased and exhibited a strong fluorescent emission intensity when the pH value was 7.0 in the physiological pH environment (pH = 7–8). The stability of Tan CDs at these pH levels was validated by this result (stable yellow intensity). When the pH was further raised to achieve an alkaline state (pH 8), the fluorescence peak changed from 430 nm to 442 nm, and the intensity of shifted fluorescence peaks decreased. Also, under extremely alkaline conditions (pH 9–10), the peak intensity decreased to a maximum level at pH 10. Changes in pH can force protonation or deprotonation and a shift of the Fermi level of the emission site in the Tan CDs core (Fig. 4c i). Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of the proposed CDs was significantly reduced under alkaline pH conditions [42,43].

Further findings showed that the fluorescence of Res changed in response to pH changes. Res was non-fluorescent at acidic pH (ranging from 4 to 6) due to the caproyl ester group that masks the -OH of resorufin and prevents fluorescence under acidic conditions [44]. At pH 6, a faint fluorescence band appeared at 592 nm, and at pH 7, the fluorescence intensity increased significantly, reaching a peak value as the pH increased to 8. In particular, in this study, the color of the Res solution was noticeably changed, changing from colorless to pink near pH 7. The peak intensity decreased as the pH increased from 9 to 10 (Fig. 5c). The pink fluorescent resorufin dye was released when the caproyl ester group was removed via ammonolysis of Res, which was attributed to the distinctive proton signal of -OH (Fig. 4c ii).

The fluorescence intensity (at ∼430 nm) of the Tan/Res CDs aqueous solution also increased with exposure to pH 4–6. In contrast, at pH 7, a new characteristic peak at 583 nm gradually appeared. The solution color of Tan/Res CDs changed from colorless to orange, along with a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the 430 nm band (Fig. 5d). Therefore, these findings support future pH monitoring applications of the proposed CDs.

The detection capability of Tan/Res CDs was also performed with various ammonia concentrations. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensity of the Tan/Res CDs gradually decreased with the increase in the concentration of NH3 from 1 to 100 µM. The quenching of fluorescence intensity confirms that Tan/Res CDs can provide colorimetric detection over a wide concentration range of NH3. Also, the calibration curve between the quenching efficiency (F/Fo) for various NH3 concentrations shows a good linear relationship (Fig. 6b) as follows:
F/Fo = −0·00383 C + 1·0056 (R2 = 0.982)

F and F0 represent the fluorescent intensity of the Tan/Res CDs with and without NH3 at ∼440 nm, and C is the ammonia concentration (μM). The limit of detection (LOD) for the Tan/Res CDs was 0.84 µM (Eq. S2, Supporting Information). The above results indicate that Tan/Res CDs are suitable for ammonia detection, confirming high accuracy and sensitivity.

Fluorescence is the process by which a photon is absorbed and then emitted. The molecular or electrical structure of pH-responsive CDs may change with pH changes, which may affect their function as sensors [45]. Modifications to the chemical structure can change the orientation or distance of fluorophore pairs acting as donor-acceptor pairs. Although the response mechanism to pH has been outlined, there has been some uncertainty about the pH sensitivity of CDs based on past findings [46].

Numerous theories have been proposed in response to this problem, including protonation and deprotonation, shifting energy levels, CD aggregation, protective shells, and proton transport [47]. However, while currently, no recognized and unambiguous mechanism for this behavior has been elucidated, other investigations have found four distinct responses (drop, increase in emission, red and blue shift) when pH is raised. Understanding the atomic structure of the prepared CDs allows the formation of a correlation between changes in pH and changes in fluorescence emission. The fluorescence intensity of Tan/Res CDs decreased steadily as the pH of the solution increased from 7–10. More specifically, the formation of new complexes can alter the fluorescence spectrum of CDs. Taken together, the lone electron pair of the amide group on the CD's surface interacts with the alkaline pH of buffer solutions and ammonia. As shown in Fig. 5d and Fig. 6a, a new fluorescence peak appeared at 588 nm by introducing alkaline pH and ammonia gas, indicating a new complex. Therefore, we confirmed that the fluorescence response of Tan/Res CDs originates from both Tan CDs, representing the color intensity and corresponding pink color due to Res under alkaline conditions. (Fig. 6c iii). When Res coexists, other functional groups on the Tan/Res CDs surface form a new complex with orange emission under alkaline conditions. Tan/Res CDs showed a characteristic orange emission that produced excellent visualization, especially in the alkaline pH region.

The pH-induced color shift of the CDs-based indicator was similar to that of the CDs solution, mainly because the CDs were successfully immobilized on the paper matrix without chemical alteration ( Fig. 7). The Res-paper changed from a pale yellowish pink to pink when the pH was higher than 7, but the light-yellow color of the Tan CDs indicator did not change color when the pH was increased from 4 to 10. Because it effectively anchors the Tan/Res CDs to the paper matrix, the Tan/Res CDs paper indicator exhibits a color change similar to that seen in the CDs’ solution. At pH 7–9, the reddish-pink color became more vivid. At pH 10, the color was at peak intensity. These findings showed that the Tan/Res CDs indicator could react to pH changes and may be used as a visual pH-sensing nanomaterial in an intelligent indicator for quick detection of food spoiling.

Fig. 7 also shows the observed color properties of the paper-based indicators. The brightness (L-value) of the Tan CDs-based indicator did not vary significantly with pH level. For example, the L-values were 82.2 at pH 4 and 84.1 at pH 8, with irregular ups and downs. The L, a, b, and ΔE values of Res and Tan/Res CDs changed when the pH was changed. However, the color characteristics of the Tan CDs indicator did not change appreciably. The a-values of the Res and Tan/Res CDs indicators increased rapidly as the pH increased from 4.0 to 10.0, while the b-values decreased significantly, indicating that the orange color intensified and the yellow tint decreased with increasing pH. In addition, the overall color difference (ΔE) of the Res and Tan/Res CDs indicators under alkaline conditions also increased dramatically, demonstrating that the indicators respond visually with high contrast to pH. Furthermore, the clear color change shown in the Tan/Res CDs indicator is said to be better suitable for identifying food spoiling when compared in terms of detectability with the naked eye. This result paved the way for further detection of BAs with Tan/Res CDs.

Animal proteins are typically abundant in fish and meat, and their deterioration is frequently attributable to microbial contamination that produces a significant amount of volatile nitrogen compounds such as dimethylamine, ammonia, and trimethylamine [48]. Ammonia can be used as a biomarker to identify food decomposition because it can simulate animal meals' protein deterioration by releasing nitrogen gas [49]. The sensitivity to pH change as a gas sensor was tested by the color change of the CDs-based paper indicators to ammonia and acid vapors ( Fig. 8). When Tan CDs-based paper indicator was exposed to acid gas, it did not change color, but it exhibited a slight, vivid yellowish tinge. Res-based paper, on the other hand, exhibited a definite pink hue when exposed to ammonia gas and a yellow color when exposed to acid vapor. Finally, the Tan/Res CDs paper indicator became brown as soon as it was subjected to ammonia vapor after remaining unchanged when exposed to acid vapor. Here, diffused volatile ammonia in the indicator combines with water to hydrolyze ammonium and hydroxyl ions, generating alkaline conditions on the indicator's surface. The hydroxyl ions subsequently react with the phenolic hydroxyl groups to modify the indicator's hue [50].

Observable color changes in the CDs indicator were consistent with fluctuations in color parameters. The color parameters of the indicator after exposure to ammonia and acid gases are shown in Fig. 8. The rising b values of the Tan CDs indicator further supported a hue shift towards yellow. However, when exposed to ammonia vapor, the a-value of the Res indicator increased while the b-value decreased significantly. Ammonia vapor dropped the b-value of the Tan/Res CDs indicator while the a-value increased, indicating a gradual shift in the indicator's color from yellow to brown. The Tan/Res CDs indicator produces ΔE values above 5, demonstrating that the color variations can be easily seen with the unaided human eye. The pH-dependent color-changing function of the Tan/Res CDs indicator shows high potential for smart packaging of meat and other protein foods, where the pH rises during storage as degradation occurs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of indicators for shrimp packaging
ce:section.ce:para: The actual food system has a more complex headspace configuration, and various metabolites interact differently with pH-sensitive indicators, so testing on target metabolites alone is insufficient to evaluate the performance of smart indicators. Therefore, in this study, we conducted a food application (shrimp) to evaluate the effectiveness of the freshness indicators. Shrimp decay by microorganisms and endogenous proteolysis, producing volatile alkaline gases (such as ammonia and BAs) during storage [51]. Therefore, shrimp tend to have higher pH values with longer storage periods, which can be detected using ammonia-sensitive indicators. Ezati and Rhim studied the application of shikonin indicator based on starch and agar polymer and reported a distinctive color change correlated with pH value in the shrimp sample [52]. Likewise, the curcumin-incorporated indicator presented very good pH-dependent color change as the quality of the shrimp changed [53].

To this end, the pH-sensitive indicators were placed on the inner side of the packaging box containing fresh shrimp without direct contact with samples (Fig. 1c), and the assembled packaging boxes were kept at 25 °C for 24 h. The color change of the indicators is shown in Fig. 9, and the color values of the indicators, shrimp's pH, and TVB-N (mg%) values are shown in Table 1. TVB-N value of the shrimp increased to 25.3 mg% after 24 h, exceeding the upper limit for edible shrimp (20 mg/100 g) [54]. As a result of the accumulation of volatile nitrogenous compounds in shrimp, the pH increased from 6.3 to 7.2 after 24 h.

The color of the Tan CDs indicator and the a- and b-values did not change significantly. While the hue of the Res indicator changed from pale pink to pink, the yellowness (b-value) decreased significantly with lower ΔE values. Nevertheless, the color change of the Res indicator was not visible to the naked eye under daylight because of the initial color of the Res solution after heat treatment. It's interesting to note that the Tan/Res CDs indicator displayed a visible color change. Tan/Res CDs indicator's color shifted from yellow to brown following spoiling, which indicates redness (a-value) was significantly improved (Fig. 9). The Tan/Res CDs indicator demonstrated a clear and noticeable color change under alkaline conditions in contrast to Tan CDs and Res indicators, demonstrating CDs' significant contribution to improving the Res-based indicator's color change. Therefore, the Tan/Res CDs indicator displays non-destructive analytical capabilities for sensing shrimp quality changes that offers sensitive, accurate, and visible detection.

Carbon dots were prepared using Tan and Res, followed by integrated filter paper to develop a pH-responsive color-changing smart indicator for detecting ammonia and meat products' freshness. Their sensitivity demonstrated the ability of the synthesized Tan/Res-CDs to detect ammonia gas and pH change. Upon interaction with alkaline pH, Tan/Res CDs exhibit a remarkable fluorescence-on response with an obvious color change (yellow to orange) which determines its potential for use in pH sensing. Importantly, the introduction of ammonia into the Tan/Res CDs quenched the fluorescent intensity, showing a low detection limit of up to 0.84 μM and displaying a good linear relationship with varying ammonia concentrations. The proposed Tan/Res CDs-derived paper indicator was applied to detect spoilage in shrimp that turned from yellow to brown, suggesting that it could be used as a real-time freshness indicator in shrimp packaging. Changes in key indicators such as pH and total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) during shrimp storage have been shown to correlate with color analysis of the Tan/Res CDs indicator. This finding suggests that Tan/Res CD-based paper indicators have many potential applications in food safety, opening huge opportunities for use in smart packaging applications as pH-sensitive perishable systems.

Parya Ezati: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Ajahar Khan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding, Acquisition, Jun Tae Kim: Supervision, Project administration, Funding Acquisition, Rahim Molaei: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. 2022R1A2B02001422).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.113013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Ezati, J.-W. Rhim, Starch and agar-based color-indicator films integrated with shikonin for smart packaging application of shrimp, ACS Food Sci. Technol. 1 (2021) 1963–1969. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsfoodscitech.1c00292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: pH-responsive pectin-based multifunctional films incorporated with curcumin and sulfur nanoparticles
Carbohydr. Polym.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Ezati, J.W. Rhim, pH-responsive pectin-based multifunctional films incorporated with curcumin and sulfur nanoparticles, Carbohydr. Polym. 230 (2020) 115638. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2019.115638.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tara gum/polyvinyl alcohol-based colorimetric NH3 indicator films incorporating curcumin for intelligent packaging
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Ma, L. Du, L. Wang, Tara gum/polyvinyl alcohol-based colorimetric NH3 indicator films incorporating curcumin for intelligent packaging, Sensors Actuators, B Chem. 244 (2017) 759–766. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.01.035.
